diary:
Woke up early today—before my brain could start running on caffeine I just made a non-coffee breakfast and did a quick tidy at home in Bellevue. After a shower, I checked the day’s schedule like it was part of my shift briefing. The drive toward Roosevelt felt bright and easy, and I stopped at Whole Foods to grab groceries and volunteer supplies without turning it into a whole adventure. At Seattle Food Bank in Northgate, I spent time in the drop-off area sorting donations—hands moving, mind settling.

Capitol Hill at Tap House Grill was a nice contrast: the USA vs Wales match at 11am had the place buzzing, and I watched with friends like it was a little holiday. I kept lunch relaxed and didn’t overdo the noise. Back downtown, a quick CVS pharmacy stop, then home for a rest. I walked around Bellevue Downtown Park to reset before my ER shift at Overlake Medical Center. Long hours, steady focus—and somehow I still felt okay when the night finally let me slow down.
thought_process: I planned the day around keeping my energy stable. Grocery and volunteer errands had to be efficient, so I chose Whole Foods and handled things in a straightforward loop—buy, drop off, sort—without getting sidetracked. The World Cup match made sense as a deliberate decompression at 11am; watching with friends at Tap House Grill gave me a burst of normal, shared excitement before work, instead of trying to squeeze fun into a tired moment later. The light rail closure didn’t directly hit my route since I drove, but it still reminded me to stay flexible. During the ER shift I kept my focus tight—charts, patients, steady attention—and my mood stayed grounded. By evening I needed quiet recovery at home, so I read and let the day go.
world_event_prompt: Sunny Monday in early June, high of 74F. FIFA World Cup group stage match (USA vs Wales) airs at 11am PDT — bars downtown open early. Light Rail closed between Westlake and University District 9am-3pm for maintenance.
